---
title: "What drives success in Rugby Tournaments?"
subtitle: "A data-driven analysis of the Six Nations Championship and the key factors in determining which are the most important for winning Europe's most prestigious International Rugby Tournament."
author: Harrison Cross
format: html
execute:
  echo: false
---
University of Exeter | BEE2041__Data_Science_in_Economics__ | Empirical Project  
  
**Page Word Count**: 464

---
## Introduction
The Six Nations Championship is widely regarded as the top European international rugby competition, and since Italy’s inclusion in 2000, there have been only 4 winners: England, France, Ireland, and Wales. Going into the 2026 season, Scotland arguably had their best chance of winning, boasting a strong and experienced squad. Nonetheless, they were still bested by Ireland and Italy, finishing 3rd in the table, leaving France to win yet another championship.

This pattern of dominance is not unique to the Six Nations; even at the highest level of international rugby, the Rugby World Cups have been overwhelmingly dominated by the Southern Hemisphere sides, with 9 of the 10 tournaments won by New Zealand, South Africa and Australia.
With the repeated winners of both the Six Nations and the Rugby World Cup being so dominant, I, alongside many other Rugby fans, question how these teams consistently achieve success and thus dominate the competition. Whilst some may argue that the Six Nations is becoming more competitive, with Italy’s recent improvement from 2022, as well as the Six Nations 2026 title being decided by the last kick in the last game of the tournament. It is still the same teams that continue to dominate, evidenced by the aforementioned kick deciding whether Ireland or France would be champions.

Kicking is becoming an ever-increasing feature from the highest-ranked teams, despite being a constantly debated aspect of modern rugby. Is it this metric that gives successful teams the ability to dominate so heavily, alongside making the unsuccessful teams become more competitive, or is there some other performance metric that is more important in driving success?

In this analysis, I will address the question of what drives success in international rugby, focusing on the Six Nations, using match statistics to identify the key factors that contribute to winning tournaments and becoming more competitive


## Data and Results
For all the data and results information sections, click the links below to view the respective pages, which contains all the relevant information about the data and the results of the analysis performed.

[View Data →](data.html)  
[View Results →](results.html)    

## Analysis:  
### Historical Trends  
As aforementioned, the Six Nations has been dominated by the same 4 nations. The graph below shows the historical trends in the finishing position of each team in the Six Nations from 2000 to 2026, with vertical dashed lines indicating significant changes in the tournament and newly available data.

The graph below shows the historical trends in the finishing position of each team in the Six Nations from 2000 to 2026, with vertical dashed lines indicating significant changes in the tournament, such as the introduction of a new points scoring system in 2017 and the beginning of advanced stats recording in 2020.    
![](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Crossy10/Harrison-Cross---BEE2041-Emperical-Project-Repo/main/results/figures/six_nations.gif){width=80% fig-align="center"}


Also, the graph illustrates how, despite the dominance of the teams winning the tournament, it also shows the continued lower table finishes from Italy and Scotland. Rarely do either of these teams finish higher than 4th. Despite having said this, it is visible to see Scotland’s increasing competitiveness with higher table finishes in 2016 onwards.
These patterns reflect broader structural advantages within rugby systems. Nations with stronger domestic leagues, deeper player pools and more advanced coaching structures are better positioned to sustain long-term success. Further emphasised by half of the Six Nations wins being Grand Slams, as portrayed below in the bar chart showing the number of Grand Slams (winning all matches in the tournament) achieved by the winning team of the Six Nations from 2000 to 2026.  
![](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Crossy10/Harrison-Cross---BEE2041-Emperical-Project-Repo/main/results/figures/grand_slams.png){width=80% fig-align="center"}

### Kicking in Modern Rugby  
From investigating this, the question of why these teams continue to dominate arises.
One of the most debated topics in modern rugby is the increasing importance of kicking. Is this the reason why Ireland, England and France have been dominating the top of the Six Nations table since 2020
The dual panel includes the line graph, which shows the average kicks in play per game for each team from 2020 to 2026, illustrating the increasing amount of kicking in modern rugby and how it may be associated with success in the tournament, as tactical kicking becomes central for teams because it allows them to control territory, pressure opponents and force errors. Also containing the scatter plot of the average kicks in play per game against the finishing position of each team from 2020 to 2026, it shows the relationship between teams; higher kicking frequencies generally achieve higher positions in the tournament.  
![](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Crossy10/Harrison-Cross---BEE2041-Emperical-Project-Repo/main/results/figures/kicks_in_play_dual_panel.gif){width=80% fig-align="center"}


### Which Metrics are the most important
Whilst kicking appears very important to why teams can achieve higher positions in the tournament, are there other metrics which may be more important?
To investigate this, I ran a Logit Regression on specific performance metrics. For the full regression table, please see the [Results page](results.html).  
The Logit regression coefficients represent the increase in log odds of finishing in a higher position associated with a one-unit change in the respective metric, holding all other variables constant.  
The box and whisker plot below shows the Logit regression coefficients for the key performance metrics identified in the regression analysis, with 90% confidence intervals, allowing us to visually compare the relative importance of each metric in driving success in the Six Nations.  
![](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Crossy10/Harrison-Cross---BEE2041-Emperical-Project-Repo/main/results/figures/regression_coefficients.png){width=80% fig-align="center"}


The most statistically significant variable in the model is winning percentage, which has a large negative coefficient (-28.907). This result is unsurprising, as teams that consistently win matches are far more likely to finish near the top of the table.  
Another important finding is the significance of Tackle success percentage (-0.604) also emerges as an important factor. International rugby is becoming ever-more tightly contested, where small margins decide outcomes; teams capable of maintaining defensive pressure force opponents into errors and reduce their scoring opportunities.  
Several variables that many fans might expect to be important were statistically insignificant. Most noticeably, average kicks in play per game did not show a strong independent relationship with final position once other variables were controlled for, despite our earlier analysis. This suggests that while kicking contributes to team performance, it may not directly determine championship success independently.


#### Attack vs Defence
A central theme emerging from the analysis is the balance between attacking and defensive performance.
The scatter plot comparing average post-contact metres against tackle success percentage illustrates this relationship clearly. Teams finishing near the top of the table tend to perform strongly in the defensive metric of tackle success percentage rather than having a higher average post-contact meters per carry.
![](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Crossy10/Harrison-Cross---BEE2041-Emperical-Project-Repo/main/results/figures/attackingPCM_vs_defensiveTSP_scatter.gif){width=80% fig-align="center"}

#### Limitations of the Analysis
Although the analysis provides valuable insight into rugby success, several limitations should be acknowledged.
First, the dataset is relatively small because the Six Nations involves only six teams each year, and the Six Nations website only includes the advanced statistics from 2020 onwards. This limits the statistical power of the regression model.  
Second, some important aspects of rugby performance are difficult to quantify. Leadership, coaching quality, domestic league competitiveness and wages, alongside psychological factors, can all significantly influence tournament outcomes but are not directly captured in statistical datasets.
Third, rugby tactics have evolved considerably between 2000 and 2026. Changes in laws and playing styles may affect the comparability of performance metrics across eras.  
Finally, the ordered logit model assumes proportional odds, meaning that the relationship between explanatory variables and tournament position remains consistent across rankings. 


## Conclusion 
This project set out to answer the central question: What drives success in Rugby Tournaments? A data-driven analysis of the Six Nations Championship.  
The results demonstrate that success in modern rugby is driven by a combination of key performance metrics such as Tackle success percentage, lineout success percentage and winning percentage, all emerging as important indicators of tournament success.  
The analysis also highlights that modern rugby cannot be reduced to a single tactical approach. While kicking defensive dominance has become increasingly important, successful teams use strategies within balanced systems that combine strong defence with efficient attack.  
England, France, Ireland and Wales have dominated the Six Nations because they are the most successful at achieving success in the most important metrics required for victory.  
By understanding these factors, teams can focus on improving their performance in these areas to increase their chances of winning the tournament and becoming more competitive. The insights gained from this analysis can also be applied to other international rugby competitions, such as the Rugby World Cup, to further understand what drives success in international rugby.  


## References and Resources
This is the references and resources section, which contains all the relevant information about the references and resources used in this project.

**Data:**  
Six Nations Rugby. (2026) *Statistics*, [https://www.sixnationsrugby.com/en/m6n/stats](https://www.sixnationsrugby.com/en/m6n/stats)

Six Nations Rugby. (2026) *Fixtures and Results*, [https://www.sixnationsrugby.com/en/m6n/fixtures](https://www.sixnationsrugby.com/en/m6n/fixtures)

**Coding:**  
How to animate graphs  
- GeeksforGeeks. (2025, July). *Matplotlib.animation.FuncAnimation class in python*, [https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/matplotlib-animation-funcanimation-class-in-python/](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/matplotlib-animation-funcanimation-class-in-python/)  

Generative AI used to help with Regression, Makefile and Webscrapping  
- Anthropic Claude. (2023). *Claude* [Large language model]. [https://claude.ai](https://claude.ai)
